feedback.text: I have decided to throw my hat into this Stop Killing games pile and contribute to it, so here we go. As time goes on video game companies tend to use more practices to squeeze every money out of the customer and make the process of maintaining games as costless as possible, some of these now being the companies treating people's game copies which they rightfully own as the company's and taking them away, shutting down the game and preventing people from playing it etc. Let me give you an example. FL Studio is a program used for creating digital music, and you need to make a one time payment to use it, that being 99$ for the Fruity (as of the time of writing). Imagine how out of place it would be if the company were to just make your copy of FL studio invalid because they decided to and acted like it isn't their fault. That is what can happen with video games (but this can happen to software in general, probably). Stop Killing Games is to make sure companies can't do whatever they want with our copies of video games and software in general, and to make sure they can use it even when the company "drops support" for it. Which brings to me to another point, if people own a copy of a video game or software and the company who made it stops distrubuting it, the people should be able- Wait, no, they should ALWAYS be able to give their copies to someone else or be able to modify their copies (and distribute these modified copies), without the company being able to prevent them from doing it. And, even when companies drop support for their video games, they should make sure they can still be played for many years to come, whether it be making it easy for people to host their own servers for multiplayer games, or any other way. Please consider adding this topic to the Digital Fairness Act.
